From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

French painter (1703–1770)

For other people named François Boucher, see [François Boucher (disambiguation)](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Boucher_(disambiguation) "François Boucher (disambiguation)").

**François Boucher** ([UK](/wiki/British_English "British English"): [/ˈbuːʃeɪ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*BOO-shay*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"), [US](/wiki/American_English "American English"): [/buːˈʃeɪ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*boo-SHAY*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"); French: [[fʁɑ̃swa buʃe]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French"); 29 September 1703 – 30 May 1770) was a French painter, draughtsman and etcher, who worked in the [Rococo](/wiki/Rococo "Rococo") style. Boucher is known for his idyllic and voluptuous paintings on classical themes, decorative allegories, and [pastoral](/wiki/Pastoral "Pastoral") scenes. He was perhaps the most celebrated painter and decorative artist of the 18th century.

## Life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fran%C3%A7ois_Boucher&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Life")]

A native of Paris, Boucher was the son of a lesser known painter Nicolas Boucher, who gave him his first artistic training. At the age of seventeen, a painting by Boucher was admired by the painter [François Lemoyne](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Lemoyne "François Lemoyne"). Lemoyne later appointed Boucher as his apprentice, but after only three months, he went to work for the engraver [Jean-François Cars](/wiki/Jean-Fran%C3%A7ois_Cars "Jean-François Cars").[[1]](#cite_note-Oxford-1)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/Marie-Jeanne_Buzeau_by_Alexandre_Roslin.jpg/250px-Marie-Jeanne_Buzeau_by_Alexandre_Roslin.jpg)](/wiki/File:Marie-Jeanne_Buzeau_by_Alexandre_Roslin.jpg)

*Portrait of Marie-Jeanne Buzeau, wife of Boucher* by [Alexander Roslin](/wiki/Alexander_Roslin "Alexander Roslin")

In 1720, he won the elite [Grand Prix de Rome](/wiki/Grand_Prix_de_Rome "Grand Prix de Rome") for painting, but did not take up the consequential opportunity to study in Italy until five years later, due to financial problems at the [Académie royale de peinture et de sculpture](/wiki/Acad%C3%A9mie_royale_de_peinture_et_de_sculpture "Académie royale de peinture et de sculpture").[[1]](#cite_note-Oxford-1) On his return from studying in Italy he was admitted to the refounded [Académie de peinture et de sculpture](/wiki/Acad%C3%A9mie_de_peinture_et_de_sculpture "Académie de peinture et de sculpture") on 24 November 1731.[[2]](#cite_note-Lev-2) His *[morceau de réception](/wiki/Reception_piece "Reception piece")* (reception piece) was his *Rinaldo and Armida* of 1734.[[2]](#cite_note-Lev-2)

Boucher married Marie-Jeanne Buzeau in 1733. The couple had three children together. Boucher became a faculty member in 1734 and his career accelerated from this point as he was promoted Professor then Rector of the academy, becoming inspector at the [Royal Gobelins Manufactory](/wiki/Gobelins_Manufactory "Gobelins Manufactory") and finally *[Premier Peintre du Roi](/wiki/Premier_Peintre_du_Roi "Premier Peintre du Roi")* (First Painter of the King) in 1765.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Resting_Girl_by_Fran%C3%A7ois_Boucher_%281753%29_-_Alte_Pinakothek_-_Munich_-_Germany_2017_%28crop%29.jpg/250px-Resting_Girl_by_Fran%C3%A7ois_Boucher_%281753%29_-_Alte_Pinakothek_-_Munich_-_Germany_2017_%28crop%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Resting_Girl_by_Fran%C3%A7ois_Boucher_(1753)_-_Alte_Pinakothek_-_Munich_-_Germany_2017_(crop).jpg)

*[The Blonde Odalisque](/wiki/The_Blonde_Odalisque "The Blonde Odalisque")* or *Resting Girl*, (thought to be [Marie-Louise O'Murphy](/wiki/Marie-Louise_O%27Murphy "Marie-Louise O'Murphy"))[[3]](#cite_note-3) c. 1752

Boucher died on 30 May 1770 in his native Paris. His name, along with that of his patron [Madame de Pompadour](/wiki/Madame_de_Pompadour "Madame de Pompadour"), had become synonymous with the French Rococo style, leading the [Goncourt brothers](/wiki/Goncourt_brothers "Goncourt brothers") to write: "Boucher is one of those men who represent the taste of a century, who express, personify and embody it."

Boucher is famous for saying that nature is "trop verte et mal éclairée" (too green and badly lit).[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Boucher was associated with the gemstone engraver [Jacques Guay](/wiki/Jacques_Guay "Jacques Guay"), whom he taught to draw. He also mentored the Moravian-Austrian painter [Martin Ferdinand Quadal](/wiki/Martin_Ferdinand_Quadal "Martin Ferdinand Quadal") as well as the neoclassical painter [Jacques-Louis David](/wiki/Jacques-Louis_David "Jacques-Louis David") in 1767.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Later, Boucher made a series of drawings of works by Guay which Madame de Pompadour then engraved and distributed as a handsomely bound volume to favored courtiers.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Painting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fran%C3%A7ois_Boucher&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Painting")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/The_secret_message%2C_by_Fran%C3%A7ois_Boucher.jpg/250px-The_secret_message%2C_by_Fran%C3%A7ois_Boucher.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_secret_message,_by_Fran%C3%A7ois_Boucher.jpg)

*The Secret Message*, 1767 ([Herzog Anton Ulrich Museum](/wiki/Herzog_Anton_Ulrich_Museum "Herzog Anton Ulrich Museum"), Braunschweig)

Boucher took inspiration from artists such as [Peter Paul Rubens](/wiki/Peter_Paul_Rubens "Peter Paul Rubens") and [Antoine Watteau](/wiki/Antoine_Watteau "Antoine Watteau").[[7]](#cite_note-7) Boucher's early works celebrate the idyllic and tranquil portrayal of nature and landscape with great elan.[[8]](#cite_note-8) However, his art typically forgoes traditional rural innocence to portray scenes with a definitive style of eroticism as his mythological scenes are passionate and intimately amorous rather than traditionally epic. Boucher's paintings of a flirtatious shepherd and shepherdess in a woodland setting, featured in *The Enjoyable Lesson* (*The Flute Players*) of 1748 and *An Autumn Pastoral* (*The Grape Eaters*) of 1749, were based upon characters in a 1745 play by Boucher's close friend Charles-Simon Favart. Boucher's characters in those paintings later inspired a pair of figurines created by the Sèvres Porcelain Manufactory, c. 1757–66.[[9]](#cite_note-9) [Marquise de Pompadour](/wiki/Madame_de_Pompadour "Madame de Pompadour") (mistress of King [Louis XV](/wiki/Louis_XV "Louis XV")), whose name became synonymous with Rococo art, was a great admirer of his work.[[10]](#cite_note-Hyde_455-10) Marquise de Pompadour is often referred to as the "godmother of Rococo"[[10]](#cite_note-Hyde_455-10) and Boucher's portraits were central to her self-presentation and cultivation of her image. For instance, Boucher's 'Sketch for a Portrait of Madame de Pompadour', displayed in the Starhemburg room at [Waddesdon Manor](/wiki/Waddesdon_Manor "Waddesdon Manor"), acts as a surviving example of the oil preparation prior to the, now lost, portrait. In one hand she holds her hat, in the other she picks up a pearl bracelet with a portrait of the king – symbolising the relationship upon which her status depends.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Virgin_and_Child_with_the_Young_Saint_John_the_Baptist_and_Angels_MET_DP123935.jpg/250px-Virgin_and_Child_with_the_Young_Saint_John_the_Baptist_and_Angels_MET_DP123935.jpg)](/wiki/File:Virgin_and_Child_with_the_Young_Saint_John_the_Baptist_and_Angels_MET_DP123935.jpg)

*[Virgin](/wiki/Mary,_mother_of_Jesus "Mary, mother of Jesus") and [Child](/wiki/Christ_Child "Christ Child") with the Young Saint [John the Baptist](/wiki/John_the_Baptist "John the Baptist") and Angels*, 1765 ([Metropolitan Museum of Art](/wiki/Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art "Metropolitan Museum of Art"))

Boucher's paintings such as *The Breakfast* (1739), a familial scene, show how he was as a master of the [genre](/wiki/Genre_works "Genre works") scene, where he regularly used his own wife and children as models. These intimate family scenes are contrasting to the licentious style seen in his *[Odalisque](/wiki/Odalisque "Odalisque")* portraits.

The dark-haired version of the *Odalisque* portraits prompted claims by the art critic [Denis Diderot](/wiki/Denis_Diderot "Denis Diderot") that Boucher was "prostituting his own wife", and the *[Blonde Odalisque](/wiki/The_Blonde_Odalisque "The Blonde Odalisque")* was a portrait that illustrated the extramarital relationships of the King. Boucher gained lasting notoriety through such private commissions for wealthy collectors and, after Diderot expressed his disapproval, his reputation came under increasing critical attack during the last years of his career.

## Theatrical and tapestry designs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fran%C3%A7ois_Boucher&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Theatrical and tapestry designs")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Fran%C3%A7ois_Boucher_-_La_Nymphe_Callisto%2C_s%C3%A9duite_par_Jupiter_sous_les_traits_de_Diane_%281759%29.jpg/250px-Fran%C3%A7ois_Boucher_-_La_Nymphe_Callisto%2C_s%C3%A9duite_par_Jupiter_sous_les_traits_de_Diane_%281759%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fran%C3%A7ois_Boucher_-_La_Nymphe_Callisto,_s%C3%A9duite_par_Jupiter_sous_les_traits_de_Diane_(1759).jpg)

*[Jupiter and Callisto](/wiki/Jupiter_and_Callisto_(Boucher,_1759) "Jupiter and Callisto (Boucher, 1759)")*, 1759 ([Nelson-Atkins Museum of Art](/wiki/Nelson-Atkins_Museum_of_Art "Nelson-Atkins Museum of Art"), Kansas City)

Along with his painting, Boucher also designed theater costumes and sets, and the ardent intrigues of the comic operas of [Charles Simon Favart](/wiki/Charles_Simon_Favart "Charles Simon Favart") closely paralleled his own style of painting. Tapestry design was also a concern. For the [Beauvais tapestry](/wiki/Beauvais_tapestry "Beauvais tapestry") workshops he first designed a series of *Fêtes italiennes* ("Italian festivals") in 1736, which proved to be very successful and often rewoven over the years, and then, commissioned in 1737, a suite of the story of [Cupid and Psyche](/wiki/Cupid_and_Psyche "Cupid and Psyche").[[11]](#cite_note-11) During two decades' involvement with the Beauvais tapestry workshops Boucher produced designs for six series of hangings in all, like [the tapestry showing Psyche and the Basketmaker from 1741 to 1742](/wiki/File:Designed_by_Fran%C3%A7ois_Boucher,_French_-_Tapestry_showing_Psyche_and_the_Basketmaker_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg "File:Designed by François Boucher, French - Tapestry showing Psyche and the Basketmaker - Google Art Project.jpg").

Boucher was also called upon for designs for court festivities organized by that section of the King's household called the [Menus-Plaisirs du Roi](/wiki/Menus-Plaisirs_du_Roi "Menus-Plaisirs du Roi") and for the opera and for royal châteaux [Versailles](/wiki/Palace_of_Versailles "Palace of Versailles"), [Fontainebleau](/wiki/Ch%C3%A2teau_de_Fontainebleau "Château de Fontainebleau") and [Choisy](/wiki/Choisy-le-Roi "Choisy-le-Roi"). His designs for all of the aforementioned augmented his earlier reputation, resulting in many engravings from his work and even reproduction of his designs on [porcelain](/wiki/Porcelain "Porcelain") and [biscuit-ware](/wiki/Biscuit-ware "Biscuit-ware") at the [Vincennes](/wiki/Vincennes_porcelain "Vincennes porcelain") and [Sèvres](/wiki/Manufacture_nationale_de_S%C3%A8vres "Manufacture nationale de Sèvres") factories. The death of [Oudry](/wiki/Jean-Baptiste_Oudry "Jean-Baptiste Oudry") in 1755 put an end to its contribution to Beauvais but his collaboration with the Gobelins lasted until 1765, when he stepped down from his position as an inspector.

## Drawings and prints

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fran%C3%A7ois_Boucher&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Drawings and prints")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Fran%C3%A7ois_Boucher_-_Young_Country_Girl_Dancing_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg/250px-Fran%C3%A7ois_Boucher_-_Young_Country_Girl_Dancing_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fran%C3%A7ois_Boucher_-_Young_Country_Girl_Dancing_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)

*Young Country Girl Dancing*, black, red and white chalk and [stump](/wiki/Stump_(drawing) "Stump (drawing)") on paper

Boucher was a prolific and varied draftsman. His drawings served not only as preparatory studies for his paintings and as designs for printmakers but also as finished works of art for which there was a great demand by collectors. Boucher followed standard studio practices of the time, by first working out the overall composition of his major canvases, and then making chalk studies for individual figures, or groups of figures. He also relied on oil and gouache sketches in the preparation of major commissions.

Gradually he made more and more sketches as independent works for the market. The [*Adoration of the Shepherds*](/wiki/File:Fran%C3%A7ois_Boucher_-_The_Adoration_of_the_Shepherds.jpg "File:François Boucher - The Adoration of the Shepherds.jpg") (Metropolitan Museum of Art), a free and painterly sketch in gouache, was long considered a preparatory sketch for Madame de Pompadour's private altarpiece *La lumière du monde* (ca. 1750, Musée des Beaux-Arts, Lyon). Recent scholarship suggests, however, that it was made at least 10 years later as an autonomous work. In the last decade of his career the artist began to favor brown chalk, a fabricated medium.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Boucher was also a gifted engraver and etcher. Boucher etched some 180 original copperplates. He made many etchings after Watteau. He thus helped propagate a taste for reproductions of drawings. When his own drawings began to sell, 266 of them were etched in stipple substitutes by [Gilles Demarteau](/wiki/Gilles_Demarteau "Gilles Demarteau"). These were printed in red ink so they resembled red chalk drawings which could be framed as little pictures. They could then be hung in the small blank spaces of the elaborately decorated paneling of luxury dwellings.

Boucher's most original inventions were decorative, and he contributed to the fashionable style of [chinoiserie](/wiki/Chinoiserie "Chinoiserie"), after having etched 12 'Figures Chinoises' (Chinese figures) by Watteau.[[13]](#cite_note-13)
